data.title: Ring Energy Eyes Production Expansion Over The Medium Term
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Ring Energy (NYSE:REI) is a Permian exploration and production company operating in the Central (CPB) and Northwest Shelf (NWS). With 85% liquids and 66% oil mix, REI produces 20.1 kboe/d (thousands of barrels of oil equivalent per day). REI has a higher-than-peers reserve life of 18 years.

In our last coverage, we focused heavily on the Stronghold acquisition and the high short interest. Since then, short interest has come down, but the stock prices has remained depressed.

Despite an increase in production of 11.5% for the 9 months ending September 2024, sales only increased 8.3% due to decreases in NGL (natural gas liquid) and natural gas prices. However, we see these pressures alleviating with the introduction of 7 Bcf/d of natural gas transit capacity from Waha to Henry Hub by 2026.

After REI reaches its deleveraging target sometime in 2026, we expect management to expand capex to increase production or target another acquisition to expand its footprint. With a current valuation of just 3.7x earnings, we believe REI is trading at a substantial discount to its fair value. Based on acquisitions at a similar production level in the CBP area, a reasonable price for REI is closer to $2.44 a share.

Estimated Fair Value

EFV (Estimated Fair Value) = EFY26 EPS (Earnings Per Share) times P/E (Price/EPS)

EFV = E26 EPS X P/E = $0.69 X 6.3 = $4.36

A P/E of 6.3 would still put REI on the lower end of independent peers. After it reaches its leverage target, we expect earnings power to be unlocked through increased volume even if hydrocarbon pricing stays depressed.

PDP (proven, developed, producing) divestitures in Texas provide insight into a theoretical short-term price for REI to be acquired or a more accurate fair market value. APA divested from its ‘non-core’ Permian assets, with a similar production and location to REI’s, for $950 million – or $45,238 boe/d (barrel of oil equivalent produced per day). Based on REI’s enterprise value, it has a valuation of $32,505 per boe/d. At the multiple of APA’s divestiture, REI is worth at least $2.44 per share.

E2025

E2026

E2027

Price-to-Sales

0.7

0.6

0.6

Price-to-Earnings

3.3

2.0

1.9

Analyst consensus data from SeekingAlpha

Operations

Total production was up 11.5% year over year for the 9 months ending September 2024, though sales revenues were only up 8.3% on a weaker pricing environment. REI’s production profile is 85% liquids and 68% oil, which is on the higher end of peers.

Total operating expenses on a per BOE basis (barrel of oil equivalent) were flat year over year for the 9 months ending September 2024. Operating income per boe declined by 6.5% year over year primarily due to a 3.0% decrease in sales price, concentrated in natural gas.

REI is in the top of the peer group for reserve life and decline rate, with around 18 years at current production levels compared to the median of 11 years. Decline rates are equally on the lower end of peers, with a rate of 25% compared to the median of 34%.

Capex for the full year 2024 is concentrated 77% on completion costs for wells, with 14% going toward improving performance of existing wells.

Costs are in line with peers, with a total completion cost for horizontals being in the $2.3-3.7 million range and verticals in the $1-1.9 million range. For the quarter ending September 2024 REI drilled 7 horizontals and 6 verticals.

The current drilling program emphasizes maintaining the current pace of operations, though we expect marginal, low-single digit increase in volumes during the last quarter of 2024 as new assets are brought online. Production curves usually peak during the first 6 months of well operation before tapering into a decline over a 24-month period. Once REI reduces its leverage ratio to below 1.0x, we expect there to be more substantial moves to expand organic production capacity.

Natural Gas Pressures Alleviating

As natural gas is a byproduct of oil production and environmental regulations prohibit flaring, many producers in the Permian are loss-taking in natural gas. However, NGL (natural gas liquids) pricing has been more resilient, allowing REI to offset the loss in natural gas. For the 9 months ending September 2024, REI’s non-oil sales narrowed to $866k from $9.1 million due to a $2.68/bbl (barrel) fall in NGL pricing and a $1.72/Mcf (thousands of cubic feet) fall in natural gas pricing.

The Waha hub, where most Permian products are aggregated, typically trades at a small discount to the Henry Hub price, where most products are exported from. The discount offsets the cost of transit and storage. Since 2018, the volume of natural gas produced in the Permian has doubled, and transit capacity to export has not kept pace. In 2024, there was a record 164 days of negative Waha gas prices, including a record low of -$7/MMBtu (millions of British thermal units) just before Labor Day weekend.

Management noted that there has been an increase in third-party uptake capacity, with the Matterhorn Express pipeline adding an additional 2.5 Bcf/d (billions of cubic feet per day) in capacity. For the week ending February 5th, 2025 the EIA reported Waha prices had increased to $1.86/MMBtu, representing a much narrower $1.36 discount to Henry Hub compared to 2024’s $2.20 average. The IEA also noted that there are another 4.5 Bcf/d coming online by 2026, which should continue to narrow the spread between Henry Hub and Waha.

A Wave of Divestitures on the Way

Management believes that there are going to be opportunities for acquisition in the area with a ‘wave of conventional targets’ from oil majors and large Permian producers. Speaking to Ion Analytics, the managing director for Houlihan Lokey stated that 18-24 months after a wave of M&A follows asset-level divestitures as firms look to maximize value. Since November 2024 in the Permian, Exxon, Permian Resources, ConocoPhillips and APA have divested a combined $3.7 billion in Permian assets deemed ‘non-core’.

We expect management to target an acquisition over the medium term, though it will likely wait until its leverage is closer to the 1.0x target, currently at 1.59x. As for size, the most recent acquisition by REI, Founders, was completed for $100 million in cash when REI’s leverage was 1.4x. We expect a marginally higher transaction size, though the EBITDA acquisition multiple will likely be higher than the 2.3x paid for Founders, with more recent deals in the area having multiples ranging from 2.5-4.0x.

Risk

As of the quarter ending September 2024, REI had no cash on its balance sheet and negative working capital. While it does have a revolver for liquidity with a $200 million undrawn component, its adjusted free cash flow has shrunk to $1.9 million quarterly, from its typical $15-20 million level due to a poor pricing environment.

While we do not expect oil prices to meaningfully decline below REI’s breakeven of approximately $35/boe, a further degradation of the hydrocarbon pricing environment would increase the expected timeframe for REI to hit its 1.0x deleveraging target and likely hamper expansion plans.

Financials

Despite the lower pricing environment, REI leads peers in net income margins, posting 39.8% during the quarter ending September 2024. Cost inflation for drilling across the United States has moderated with fewer than 20% of firms reporting cost increases during the quarter ending September 2024. REI’s costs, as previously discussed, have remained flat.

Management has said that it is ‘more focused on debt reduction than trying to grow the company’ now. Over the trailing twelve months, its debt to adjusted EBITDA (adding back share comp and non-cash retirement costs) is at 1.59x. The current target ratio before it begins expansionary efforts again is 1.0x. At the current pace of repayments, $15 million quarterly, it will be at the target in the middle of 2026. The current weighted average interest on the revolver component of the debt is 9.3%, with total quarterly interest cost being $10.8 million, or around 12.5% of revenues.

It is possible that repayments increase with a more aggressive offloading of assets operating at higher than target production costs, or a more favorable oil pricing environment. During the quarter ending September 2024, REI sold some ‘non-core’ rigs for $5.5 million in cash.

Conclusion

Though REI does have a higher debt load than peers, it has put itself on the path for hitting its deleveraging target by the middle of 2026. If hydrocarbon prices begin to climb in the latter half of 2025, it could also provide a much-needed boost to free cash for faster repayment. By that point, we expect divestiture activity by majors and independents in the Permian to reach its peak, which should give REI the opportunity to purchase more PDP assets at a good price or invest in organically expanding production.

Despite a clear path to higher production and earnings, the stock has remained flat over the trailing twelve months and trades at just 3.7x earnings. Similar assets have divested for substantially higher prices, and thus we believe REI is undervalued at its current price.

data.title: Why I'm Pressing Pause: Downgrading Broadcom To Hold Amid Valuation Concerns
data.description:
Since I last wrote about Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) (NEOE:AVGO:CA) on December 19, 2024, with a buy recommendation, the stock has survived the DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) scare and is up a little over 7% compared to the S&P 500's (SPX) 3.5% rise. However, even before DeepSeek came onto the scene, the stock had begun to flatline, likely because the market had started paying more attention to the company's valuation and risks.

With a new Presidential administration, the geopolitical risks concerning China have increased. This risk could adversely impact Broadcom, including increased scrutiny from Chinese regulators on business practices and future deals. The company could also face increased tariffs from China or export restrictions from the U.S., which could reduce its revenue. Broadcom has several large Chinese customers, including ByteDance (BDNCE) (owner of TikTok), with whom news organizations have reported that it is co-developing an advanced AI chip. Because of the trade friction, some Chinese-based customers like ByteDance may switch to Chinese chip companies.

Although management has positioned the company well in the AI era, and DeepSeek may eventually spark a revolution of cost efficiency in AI, potentially benefiting Broadcom, investors should exercise caution before investing. This article will discuss how its VMware acquisition could help the company's Artificial Intelligence ambitions. It will also discuss what to look for when the company reports first quarter fiscal year ("FY") 2025 earnings on March 6, 2025. Last, the article will examine the stock's valuation, risks, and why I am downgrading the stock to a Hold.

Broadcom's acquisition of VMware could help it compete in AI

For quite some time, people have viewed Broadcom's acquisition of software companies like Computer Associates and Symantec as a head-scratcher. Some can't see the purpose of combining a software company with a hardware company. That view was no different when Broadcom showed interest in acquiring VMware in 2022. Some questioned the deal due to the lack of synergies between the two companies and the challenges of integrating VMware's business with Broadcom's existing operations. Despite some resistance to the deal, Broadcom completed its acquisition of VMware on November 22, 2023.

When people look back on Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Hock Tan's move into software several years from now, they will likely call him prescient and visionary. Successful technology companies are no longer stuck to being just hardware or software companies; instead, they are becoming more infrastructure technology companies encompassing both hardware and software to differentiate in a highly competitive market. VMware helps Broadcom stand out in an era where Enterprises no longer store data in one central location. Today, companies have computer assets worldwide, running various servers and performing different tasks. The problem that quickly arose when operating this way was that some computers may be operating near maximum capacity, and some computers may be idle—highly inefficient. Another problem was that companies could only use some servers to run specific software.

VMware creates a more efficient computing operation by using software to combine disparate computing resources into one massive supercomputer—a technology named virtualization. You might hear people refer to vSphere, which is its virtualization technology. Enterprises save money using VMware by cutting electrical costs by using fewer physical servers and gaining the flexibility to run virtually any software in any location, including private data centers, branch offices, and the public cloud. This flexibility is handy for Information Technology ("IT") teams to deploy, monitor, and maintain AI applications. VMware's virtualization technology also helps AI applications run more effectively, enabling faster training and inference. Another huge benefit is that virtualization technology provides security features across all on-premise (on-prem) private, third-party-hosted private, and public clouds. No matter where a company's developers or IT teams deploy an AI application and data, it will have protection.

Although management hasn't explicitly stated it as a goal, their actions and focus suggest Broadcom's long-term vision is for customers to train and run AI models within customer-owned private data centers on VMware's platform. Enterprises may buy into this vision because it may solve several significant issues, including data privacy, security, regulatory compliance, and possibly lower costs for large-scale AI deployments. However, the more likely scenario is that customers will prefer a hybrid approach to AI, using a mixture of private and public clouds.

Although NVIDIA (NVDA) and Broadcom are competitors, the two companies established a collaboration in 2023 named VMware Private AI Foundation. A product announcement blog stated:

VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA: VMware and NVIDIA are announcing our plans to collaborate to develop a fully integrated Generative AI platform called VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA. This platform will enable enterprises to fine-tune LLM models and run inference workloads in their data centers, addressing privacy, choice, cost, performance, and compliance concerns. The platform will include the NVIDIA NeMo™ framework, NVIDIA LLMs, and other community models (such as Hugging face models) running on VMware Cloud Foundation.

If this collaboration is successful, VMware Private AI could become the de facto standard platform for running Enterprise AI hybrid cloud workloads, which could greatly benefit Broadcom's AI hardware ambitions. However, at this point, that is only an aspiration.

The more companies use VMware Private AI, the higher the demand for the underlying high-performance servers, networking, and storage that power the private cloud service. Since Broadcom provides many server, networking, and storage components, it could indirectly benefit the company's hardware segment. For instance, it could make its hardware attractive to customers by optimizing and tightly integrating its customized AI XPU chips, storage, and networking products with VMware to maximize speed, efficiency, and costs for customers with private clouds. NVIDIA also wins with VMware Private AI Foundation because the VMware platform integrates deeply with NVIDIA DGX cloud (a public cloud) and AI management tools. VMware Private AI customers can also access NVIDIA NeMo, a framework for building, customizing, and deploying generative AI models.

What I am about to say is speculation. Still, I see how this collaboration can deepen to the point where the two companies create a hybrid computing platform that uses Broadcom's XPUs and GPUs for the tasks for which each chip is best suited. NVIDIA's GPUs are best for general-purpose AI model training and inference, while XPUs are best when low latency is needed, such as in real-time AI applications and high-frequency trading.

Although Broadcom's stock dropped due to the Chinese DeepSeek news, if the AI industry moves toward more efficiency, the company's acquisition of VMware could benefit Broadcom if it establishes VMware Private AI as a standard hybrid computing platform for AI.

What to expect when Broadcom reports first quarter 2025 results

In my last article on Broadcom, I delved into the company's fourth-quarter results. Although the company missed analysts' fourth-quarter revenue estimates (the first revenue miss in the last 12 quarters) and issued first-quarter fiscal year FY 2025 revenue guidance of $14.6 billion, slightly below consensus analysts' estimates of $14.62 billion before earnings, the stock rose around 24% the day after issuing fourth-quarter results. Considering the market's positive reaction to the results, investors likely focused on the company's robust revenue growth rather than the small miss.

Broadcom's fourth-quarter revenue increased 51% year over year, which is excellent growth. Even excluding VMware, revenue grew by 11%, which is healthy organic growth. The market was pleased that much of the company's revenue rose due to demand for AI semiconductors and VMware. Fourth-quarter Infrastructure Software revenue growth increased 196% year over year, mainly due to VMware. Investors were pleased with this result and the news that the company was close to successfully integrating VMware into its core operations. CEO Hock Tan said on the fourth quarter 2024 earnings call:

The integration of VMware is largely complete. Revenue is on a growth trajectory and operating margin reached 70% exiting 2024. We are well on the path to delivering incremental adjusted EBITDA at a level that significantly exceeds the $8.5 billion we communicated when we announced the deal. We are planning to achieve this much earlier than our initial target of three years.

The company's AI revenue, primarily from the robust demand for custom AI accelerators (XPUs) and networking, grew 220% from $3.8 billion in fiscal 2023 to $12.2 billion in fiscal 2024. At the end of its fiscal 2024, 41% of Semiconductor revenue came from AI. CEO Tan said about Semiconductor revenue on the fourth quarter earnings call (emphasis added):

On the broad portfolio of non-AI semiconductors with its multiple end-markets, we saw a cyclical bottom in fiscal 2024 at $17.8 billion. We expect a recovery from this level at the industry's historical growth rate of mid-single digits. In sharp contrast, we see our opportunity over the next three years in AI as massive. Specific hyperscalers have begun their respective journeys to develop their own custom AI accelerators or XPUs, as well as network these XPUs with open and scalable Ethernet connectivity. For each of them, this represents a multi-year, not a quarter-to-quarter journey.

When the company announces its first quarter 2025 results, please pay close attention to what management says about AI hardware revenue and VMware, its two primary growth drivers. The stock price will likely increase if those two growth areas remain healthy. On the other hand, if either area shows deteriorating growth or the market begins taking seriously some risk to future revenue growth in these areas, the stock could decline.

Although first-quarter guidance was slightly below analysts' estimates, it still represents a solid 22% year-over-year growth. Management forecasts that the adjusted EBITDA margin will expand 100 bps sequentially to 66% in the first quarter of 2025, indicating healthy profitability. The company forecasts that semiconductor revenue will grow 10% to $8.1 billion in the first quarter of FY 2025, compared to the previous year's comparable quarter, and infrastructure software revenue will grow 41% year over year to $6.5 billion.

Risks

Even though Broadcom has largely integrated VMware, there are still risks that the acquisition may not turn out well. VMware's customers may become dissatisfied with Broadcom's direction for the company. This risk is not just theoretical. Ars Technica recently published an article that stated:

Broadcom's ownership of VMware has discouraged many of its customers, as companies are displeased with how the trillion-dollar firm has run the virtualization business since buying it in November 2023. Many have discussed reducing or eliminating ties with the company.

Some of the issues that turn customers off to VMware are the switch from perpetual licenses to a subscription model, the increasing prices due to unwanted bundled products, worse customer service, and changes to its partner program that could eliminate some valuable VMware partners for some customers.

There is also the risk that VMware will become a worse service over time. Broadcom is a serial acquirer with a reputation for cost-cutting with its acquisitions. Investors often like the resulting improved profitability. Still, the downside is that cost-cutting could lead to loss of key personnel, decreased investment spending, and slower development of new features and products. VMware is not the only company in the virtualization market. A competent rival like Nutanix (NTNX) may supersede it if VMware becomes less competitive. Two Seeking Alpha analysts have already written about Nutanix taking market share from VMware here and here. So, Broadcom-VMware's dreams of becoming the standard platform for running Enterprise AI hybrid cloud workloads could potentially fade if it cannot stem the losses of large customers to Nutanix. Additionally, other players in the virtualization market may take market share away from VMware, including Hewlett Packard Enterprise's (HPE) SimpliVity, Microsoft's (MSFT) Azure Local, and smaller competitors like StarWind and Scale Computing, which states on its website, "Planning your VMware Exit? Save 25% with the Industry-Leading VMware Alternative."

Suppose VMware produces less growth than expected or fails to help Broadcom's AI hardware solutions proliferate. In that case, the company may fail to deliver the revenue growth, profitability, and FCF that investors need to justify its valuation.

I recently wrote an article about Marvell (MRVL), which makes similar custom AI chips and networking equipment. Some see Marvell as a more innovative company that capitalizes on trends faster than Broadcom. Marvell could potentially slow Broadcom's AI hardware ambitions just enough to prevent it from reaching the growth rates the company expects over the long term. Additionally, Broadcom faces many more agile, smaller competitors in the AI hardware space that could disrupt the company if it fails to innovate quickly. These smaller competitors include Groq, SambaNova Systems, and SoftBank Group (OTCPK:SFTBY)-backed Graphcore.

Valuation

Broadcom's trailing 12-month ("TTM") price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is well above its three-, five-, seven-, and ten-year median P/S ratios, indicating that the market may overvalue the stock. However, this backward-looking metric may not reflect improved growth prospects. If the company is entering a higher growth phase powered by AI, the stock could deserve to trade above its median.

Broadcom's TTM price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio is 180.65, well above the IT sector's median P/E of 33.00 and five-year average P/E of 52.59. Based on this valuation method, some might conclude that the market overvalues stock. However, a high P/E doesn't necessarily mean overvaluation; it does signal that investors have high expectations for earnings growth, which may be warranted considering the company's AI opportunities. Additionally, people should remember that the VMware acquisition decreased earnings-per-share ("EPS") due to significantly increasing operating expenses, higher interest expenses (Broadcom added $8 billion of debt to acquire VMware), and restructuring and integration costs. CEO Tan is a ruthless cost-cutter; if he acts as he has done historically by reducing operating costs and paying down debt (reducing interest expenses), Broadcom's EPS should improve, helping lower the P/E (assuming a stable stock price).

Broadcom's FY 2025 PEG ratio is 1.2 (FY 2025 P/E of 36.66 divided by analysts' estimated EPS growth of 30.55%). Its FY 2026 PEG ratio is 1.59 (FY 2026 P/E of 30.73 divided by analysts' estimated EPS growth of 19.30%). Generally, the market will allow a PEG ratio to reach 2.0 before considering it overvalued. According to that convention, the market may undervalue the company's FY 2025 and FY 2026 earnings growth.

If the company sold at an FY 2025 PEG ratio of 2.0, the stock price would be $388.60, a 66.75% rise over its February 14 closing price of $233.04. Assuming an FY 2026 PEG ratio of 2.0, the stock price would be $292.59, a $25.55% rise over the current stock price.

Generally, the market considers high free cash flow ("FCF") yields a sign of undervaluation and low FCF yields a sign of overvaluation. Investors may allow high-growth tech stocks like NVIDIA, Broadcom, and Marvell's FCF yield to go as low as 1.0% before considering them overvalued. Since Broadcom has the highest FCF yield of 1.76% on the chart above, on a relative valuation basis, the market may undervalue Broadcom.

Broadcom's price-to-FCF of 56.67 is well above three-, five-, seven-, and ten-year medians. Some may consider the stock overvalued based on where it trades compared to its medians. An alternative view is that the market has high expectations for the company's FCF growth, and if it reaches those expectations, the market may value the stock appropriately.

Downgrading Broadcom to hold

Several traditional valuation metrics indicate overvaluation; Seeking Alpha's quant rates the valuation an F. The company also has risks that could disrupt its plans. If the company fails to achieve the market's expected EPS and FCF, the stock's decline could be steep at the current valuation. As a result, risk-averse investors should avoid the stock. Even true believers in the company's AI opportunity should be mindful of the significant risks of investing in this company. Analysts' high expectations for EPS growth this year may enchant some investors, but several risks could derail that growth. Only highly aggressive investors comfortable with substantial risk should consider buying shares as a speculative investment at the current valuation. I downgrade the stock to hold.

data.title: Apple Could Show Big Correction As iPhone Reality Sets In
data.description:
Apple’s (NASDAQ:AAPL) (TSX:AAPL:CA) recent earnings showed several worrying trends that can build a negative sentiment toward the stock in the next few quarters. Despite the massive hype for Apple Intelligence, the iPhone segment has shown a 1% YoY decline. The Greater China region reported an 11% YoY revenue decline, which follows another 13% YoY revenue decline in the year-ago quarter. On the other hand, China’s Huawei has reported 22% YoY revenue growth in 2024. This shows a clear market share decline for Apple in Greater China, which makes up 15% of its gross sales. In the previous article, it was mentioned that the regulatory headwinds can get stronger, which will hurt the sentiment in the near term.

The recent tariff wars can also hurt Apple sales if the company passes the higher costs to customers. The revenue growth of Apple is already very modest. Over the last three years, Apple’s revenue base has increased by a cumulative 2% which does not keep pace with the inflation. The net income has also declined by 5%. Most of the stock price growth in the last three years has been due to a higher PE multiple which has made the stock more expensive than other faster-growing big tech companies like Meta (META) and Alphabet (GOOG).

Apple Intelligence fails to live up to the hype

There was massive excitement around Apple Intelligence and the latest iPhone iteration. Due to the rapid growth in new AI tools, it was expected that customers would be eager to upgrade their Apple devices to take advantage of Apple Intelligence. However, this is not reflected in the recent earnings report. Apple’s iPhone segment reported 1% YoY decline in the recent quarter.

Figure: Apple's revenue by segment. Source: Apple Filings

The iPhone segment still makes up over 50% of the revenue base of Apple. The management has mentioned that the lack of Apple Intelligence in Greater China led to lower sales in the region which pulled down the overall revenue of this segment. However, even if we take out the headwinds in the Greater China region, the remaining regions would have likely seen low-single-digit iPhone growth of 1%-3%. This is very far from the earlier expectation that AI tools could help Apple deliver another upgrade supercycle.

Greater China headwinds need to be priced in

Apple reported 11% YoY revenue decline in Greater China in the recent quarter. The revenue in Greater China fell to $18.5 billion from $20.8 billion in the year-ago quarter. The revenue decline in Greater China was more than the revenue increase in the entire Americas region. This shows the importance of Greater China which still contributes 15% of the gross revenue.

Figure: Decline in Greater China revenue. Source: Apple Filings

The recent decline follows another 13% YoY decline in revenue reported by Apple in Greater China in the year-ago quarter. Hence, Apple has seen its revenue decline by almost a quarter in the last two years. This is a big decline for one of the key regions of Apple. Some analysts have commented that other regions like India or Southeast Asia might make up for the dip in sales in China. However, Apple’s YoY growth in Rest of Asia Pacific region was also only 1% from a lower base. This shows that the headwinds in China are too big for Apple.

Figure: Decline in Apple’s China revenue in the year-ago quarter. Source: Apple Filings

China’s telecom giant Huawei has reported stunning results despite strong sanctions against the company. In the recent filings, Huawei reported revenue of $118 billion in 2024 which is a 22% YoY growth.

Figure: Rapid growth in market share of Huawei. Source: Canalys

Huawei could also expand its presence in premium segment of other emerging markets where it would like to increase its market share. This would be in direct competition with Apple.

Tariffs and probes in App Store

The recent tariffs announced by the White House could be another major headwind for Apple. In a recent report, Bank of America Securities analyst estimated that Apple could see up to 12 cent decline in EPS due to tariffs which is equal to 1.8% of Apple’s full-year EPS. There is always a possibility of restrictions on Apple by Chinese administration which can cause further challenges for the company’s business in the region.

China has also ordered a probe into App Store fees and the high commission charged by Apple. We have already seen European Commission probe App Store practices and other regulators could follow this example. Apple’s App Store revenue stream is a very high gross margin business and any headwind on this could cause a significant downward revision in Apple’s EPS. This will certainly hurt the stock sentiment as the stock is already quite expensive when we compare with other peers.

Low growth, high valuation multiple

Apple is priced as if it will deliver very strong growth in earnings and revenue. However, the company has shown stagnant revenue in the last three years and is facing headwinds in key business segments. Apple stock is trading at 31 times the EPS estimate for fiscal year ending Sep 2025, and it is trading at 28 times the EPS estimate for fiscal year ending Sep 2026. On the other hand, Alphabet is trading at only 19 times. Alphabet has a higher forward EPS growth projection.

Figure: EPS estimate of Apple for the next two years. Source: Seeking Alpha

Apple will be at the center of any tariff war or regulatory headwind. If App Store or licensing revenue faces pressure from any regulator, we could see a significant downward revision in forward EPS estimates of Apple.

Figure: Revenue growth in Apple, Alphabet and Meta in the last three years.

The lack of growth, headwinds in China, and regulatory challenges make Apple stock very expensive at the current price. The management has announced spending $30 billion on buybacks and dividends in this quarter. This is a positive for the stock, but it will still equate to less than 3% yield for investors at the current stock price.

Investor Takeaway

Apple Intelligence has failed to deliver the expected results as iPhone sales declined by 1% YoY. Apple’s Greater China YoY revenue has declined by 11% after another 13% YoY decline in the year-ago quarter. Greater China still contributes 15% of the company’s revenue base, and this business is showing big challenges as Huawei and other competitors increase their market share.

Apple will also face challenges due to tariffs and regulatory headwinds for the lucrative App Store business. We could see EPS downward revisions in the next few months if any of these challenges impact the revenue. At the same time, Apple stock is trading at a PE multiple of 28 for fiscal year ending Sep 2026 compared to 24 times for Meta and only 19 for Alphabet. This makes Apple stock very expensive and could lead to a correction in the stock in the near term.

data.title: IWM: Small Caps Need Something Radical, Enter DOGE
data.description:
My last article on small caps was published in October 2024 and argued that the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (NYSEARCA:IWM) could make a new all-time high based on favorable underlying conditions.

A slow but stable economy and slow easing cycle from the Fed should lead to a slow but steady rally.

A new high was made in November, but the rally could not sustain the gains. The reason was perhaps counter-intuitive but was explained in my October article.

A strong economy and an aggressive Fed cutting cycle would be ideal for IWM, but they are not mutually exclusive. It may be better off with neither.

Data has picked up since the Fed started cutting rates. Consequently, inflation is rising again, and the Fed is back to a 'higher for longer' stance. This article looks at the prospects for IWM under these new conditions and how radical new fiscal policy could change them.

IWM - A Benchmark Rather than an Attractive Investment

There is nothing sophisticated about the composition of IWM. It simply selects the smallest 2000 companies out of the Russell 3000 which comprises 98% of the investable US equity market. You can probably already imagine what kind of companies make the list, and here is confirmation -

Nearly half the companies are unprofitable. This makes stats on PE ratio and EPS growth unreliable.

The smallest companies have market caps of around $50M, the largest near $16B. Yet, they are all mixed into one fund and weighted by float-adjusted capitalization.

As the fund page states, IWM "provides an unbiased, complete view" of the small-cap segment. Whether it is something you would want to invest in it is another matter, though. The whole small-cap space has struggled in recent years, and other funds have provided superior returns with small-cap growth (IWO) and active management (AFSM) outperforming.

IWM Outlook

IWM does provide a useful benchmark for the space. As the intro outlined, its outlook has turned more negative as growth and inflation have picked up, causing the Fed to shift back to a "higher for longer" stance on rates. The graphic below is a summary of the Fed's summary of economic projections (SEP) from December compared to the previous release from September.

The Fed has a tough time keeping everything in balance, and it seems the 100bps of cuts they have already delivered may have tipped the scales too much towards growth and inflation. Both metrics were revised higher slightly in December and caused 50bps fewer cuts to be projected in 2025.

Last week's CPI readings for January underlined this point, as the headline MoM reading came in at a shocking 0.5%. This happened before any of Trump's expected inflationary policies came into effect, so we can attribute this inflation "bump" (as Chair Powell called it) to the Fed. It will make them extremely cautious to cut again.

"Higher for longer" is bad news for IWM. As shown earlier, 42% of companies make no profits and likely rely on credit in one form or another. Most small companies need to take on debt to grow or to survive. High borrowing costs are felt more in this segment of the equity market than in any other.

IWM may be a fund to avoid until the metrics shown above are back in better balance. It is important growth does not completely flatline, as a recession would hurt small companies, but mild positive growth in the 1-2% range would be ideal. Steady unemployment in the 4.3-4.4% range would be neither too tight nor concerningly loose. As for inflation - the lower, the better - but a 2-2.5% range would be ideal and about all we could expect if growth and employment stay healthy.

What IWM really needs to see is a clear signal the Fed want to get away from restrictive rates and back to a neutral level, estimated to be around 3%. Under current conditions, this is very unlikely. Even if the growth/labour market/inflation were better balanced, it would likely be a slow process as the Fed will be cautious about cutting too fast. It may take something radical to shake up the landscape to a positive one for IWM.

Enter DOGE

DOGE is about as radical as fiscal policy gets. Setting up a new department run by a CEO and the world's richest man and giving it almost unlimited authority to cut government waste sounds fanciful. But it's happening, and the cuts have been widespread already. This is shaking up the whole equation outlined above.

For a start, unemployment is rising.

Secondly, growth is likely to cool.

Thirdly, and this is what could benefit IWM the most, government deficits will fall. This means less issuance of USTs, and lower yields. This may be one reason why the yield curve has moved lower this year despite inflation moving higher.

In theory, DOGE could be a positive driver for IWM. However, it's not something that will have an immediate effect and there are question marks over what it will do to growth and other areas. I still think IWM is a fund to avoid for now, but it is worth keeping it on your watchlist as the environment could be changing.

Conclusions

IWM has fallen back after making a new all-time high in November 2024. The current mix of strengthening data, hot inflation, and a higher for longer Fed policy are not ideal for small caps, and it does not look like changing very quickly. However, DOGE may change all that and its actions could lead to lower rates and a looser Fed policy sooner rather than later. I think IWM is a fund to "hold" rather than "buy" right here and now, but I like the fact it has a potential positive driver and will keep monitoring it closely.

data.title: Fair Isaac: Score Prices Shall Keep Rising
data.description:
Introduction

Since my last Fair Isaac Corporation (NYSE:FICO) article "FICO: A Buy On Pullbacks" published in November 2023 with a buy rating, the stock has more than doubled its share price due to the outstanding performance of the business and a multiple expansion.

As stated in the comment section of the previous article, I sold half of my position at $2,350, given the share price was too far away from my estimate of the fair price on a base case scenario, which I am updating in this article.

Although I consider the company to be slightly overvalued, I don't believe in selling the highest-quality companies (and FICO ranks #1 in my watchlist quality wise). That is why I keep half of my position (around 12% of my portfolio) and will increase again if the share price falls below its fair value.

Company Overview

FICO operates in two business segments, scores and software. While both segments have outstanding margins and low need to reinvest due to their asset light nature (D&A was 0.75% of FY2024's revenues), the worst of its segments is the software one with operating margins above 30%. The score segment operating margins have ranged between 86% and 90% during the last four quarters, and it enjoys significant operating leverage.

Scores Segment

FICO owns the proprietary algorithm applied to consumers' data that generates the FICO Score (a three-digit number ranging from 300 to 850 that determines the customer's credit quality).

Since the data is collected and managed by the consumer reporting agencies (Experian, TransUnion, and Equifax), the only costs for FICO are the ones related to improving the predictability of its scores (the model has been updated several times since its first version was released in 1989).

The cost of issuing an extra score is zero, and FICO doesn't need to deal with the final customer, since banks, credit card issuers, mortgage lenders and auto loan originators deal with the credit bureaus, paying a percentage of the fee to FICO in exchange of applying its algorithm.

Most of the revenues on the score segment are generated B2B (∼77%), and FICO's fee represents a minimal cost compared to the overall cost of a lending process.

The main strengths of the scores segment are the network effects, and the fact it has become an industry standard on loan originations. On top of that, when a mortgage lender wants to sell the loan to the GSE (Government Sponsored Agencies like Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac), it is mandatory to provide the FICO score.

Mortgage originations represent 34% of the scores' revenue (Q1 2025), and FICO has recently announced a price increase from $3.5 in 2024 to $4.95 in 2025. That is a 41% price increase on top of many previous price increases. From the $0.5-$0.6 per pull charged in 2018, I've approximated the mortgage score price per year, obtaining a CAGR of over 38%.

The reasoning behind this price increases is the high value provided by the FICO score and the fact the company has held it at abnormal low prices for decades. Given the strong competitive position, and the low price compared to the overall mortgage origination fees, the company has been able to push those price increases.

In the expected growth section, I will discuss how much I believe the company will be able to increase its prices, which, in my opinion, is the most important factor in determining its fair price and the future growth.

Scores Segment Recent Performance

During the last year (Q2 2024 to Q1 2025), FICO has generated $963.3M in revenues in the scores segment, with a YoY increase of 23% in its last quarter.

The most notable developments by sub-segments are:

  • Mortgage origination revenues were up 110% YoY.
  • Auto origination revenues were up 5% YoY.
  • Credit card and personal loan revenues were down 3% YoY.
  • B2C revenues were up 3% YoY.

Note that even the mortgage originations have doubled YoY, this series of data presents significant volatility, and it needs to be taken with a grain of salt. Despite this, mortgage originations have increased after a softer market due to the initial rate increases in 2022, and it seems to be normalizing.

If the trend continues its current course, and given the recent price increase, I expect significant growth in the B2B side of the business.

On the B2C side of the business, even the company doesn't disclose the exact revenues generated, by reviewing past earnings calls one can see that the variance is much lower, mainly driven by the number of pulls from individual customers through the myFICO.com website.

Another relevant development during the last months has been the increasing adoption of FICO 10T (which is expected to be required for government backed mortgages sold to the GSEs, but there is no specific timeline). Recently, non-government backed mortgages utilizing FICO 10T scoring model began trading on MCT Marketplace, the largest U.S. mortgage secondary market. I believe this is a highly relevant development that keeps the FICO models as the industry standard.

Software Segment

While the scores part of FICO's business has been relatively linear over the decades product-wise, the other segments have been changing significantly with some divestitures and new business lines.

The software segment provides analytics, decision-making, and fraud detection. The company has transitioned from having a long list of applications to an integrated cloud-based platform where customers can bring data together from multiple sources and tailor its configuration to a high degree based on their needs. Currently, the FICO Platform generates 31% of its annual recurring revenues (ARR).

The main drivers for growth in this segment are the increase in the number of users (mainly banks, insurers, lenders, large retailers, government and public agencies, telecommunications providers, and automotive companies) and the increase in usage, since FICO subscribes multi-year contracts with partial payments based on the number of accounts, transactions or decisions use cases deployed.

Software Segment Recent Performance

During the last quarter, the growth in the software segment has reduced its pace, as shown in the image below:

The ARR on the platform segment is currently growing at 20%, which is significantly lower compared to the previous quarters, bringing the overall software growth rates to 6% YoY (8% if eliminating the currency impact), generating annual revenues of $729M.

The 6% growth has been mainly driven by the increase in usage for current customers (∼83% of the increase), and the rest has been driven by an increase in customer base.

In my personal view, this is the most negative development during the last months, since the increase in usage is growing slower along with the customer base growth.

Regarding this part of the business, during the last earnings call the management stated the following:

I wouldn't read too much into any particular quarter. This quarter, we got dinged a little bit with foreign exchange. But really, the biggest driver is the fact that it flows through from bookings and we had some weaker bookings quarters in the past. We haven't revised our view about the kind of long-term growth trajectory for the platform business, which is in the 30s, 30% or so. And so I wouldn't read too much into this quarter's number.

(William Lansing, CEO)

While the management expects ARR to accelerate in the coming quarters, and I have FICO's management in high regard due to its conservative guidance and proven track record of delivering results, I will keep track of the growth rates in the software segment during the next quarters but will adjust my model's assumptions.

Transition from tri-merge to bi-merge

The transition from the tri-merge reporting when applying for a mortgage to the bi-merge report has been discussed for a long time. In 2022, the Federal Housing Finance Authority (FHFA) announced they are planning to shift from requiring three credit reports (one for every agency) to requiring only two but providing on top of the FICO 10T Score, also the Vantage 4.0 (a model developed by the three major credit bureaus).

While it was initially planned for implementation during the first quarter of 2024, it has been constantly delayed, and the FHFA has recently announced there is no planned timeline.

The intention for this change was to increase competition between the credit bureaus, but the industry fears that providing only two reports would reduce the visibility of the customer's credit risk, and its implementation would have a transition that could increase costs for borrowers.

One of the key competitive advantages of FICO and the credit agencies is the reduced cost compared to the mortgage closing costs.

According to a report released by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau in September 2023, the average closing cost for a mortgage was around $6,000.

A tri-merger report costs around $90 ($30 per report), of which $14.85 will go to FICO in 2025. This is a 1.5% total cost for the borrower, and FICO is charging 0.25% of the mortgage closing costs.

Given the relatively low percentage charged by FICO and the three bureaus, I believe the savings provided by this transition doesn't compensate for the costs.

If the bi-merge report was implemented, after the transition period the number of FICO scores would temporarily decrease by about 33%, which is the price increase for one year. In that scenario, it'd be important to follow the magnitude of the stock price drop to see if the market is exaggerating the consequences.

Expected Growth and Valuation

As stated before, I consider the current price to be overvalued, and in this section, I will present my assumptions in a base case scenario, along with an optimistic and a pessimistic scenario along with its chances of occurring.

Base Case Scenario

In my base case scenario, I make the following assumptions:

  • B2C Scores growing at 3.5%: in line with inflation with modest volume growth.
  • B2B Score price growing at 14.5% CAGR (reaching 0.5% of mortgage closing costs in 2032, assuming a 3.5% increase in mortgage closing costs per year).
  • B2B volumes continuing its accelerated growth given the low comparables and normalizing in 3 years.
  • The software segment growing at 5% per year.
  • Margin increase in scores and software due to operating leverage.
  • A tax rate decrease to 15% between 2026 and 2029, and increase again from 2030.
  • An 8.5% discount rate.
  • A 4% growth in perpetuity and 25x FCF exit multiple.
  • A 14% decrease in shares outstanding during the next 8 years, given buybacks are the main use for FICO's excess free cash flow.

In my base case scenario, I assume no further regulations or limits in price increases, and growth rates that, I believe, are neutral in B2B volume growth and software.

The long-term growth rate is consistent with inflation, and the FCF exit multiple is reasonable for a company with FICO's characteristics but assuming a much slower growth.

The current average fair price between the exit multiple and growth in perpetuity approaches is $1,510, implying the stock is currently overvalued by 20%.

Under these assumptions, the stock would have a share price of $3,071 in 2032, providing an 8% yearly return from current prices, which is significantly below my hurdle rate.

Optimistic and Pessimistic Scenarios

On an optimistic scenario, I expect the score segment to grow at 4% per year and the B2B segment to increase its prices to 0.75% of the total mortgage closing price (reaching $19 per score in 2023 from the current $4.95). The volume growth I used in this scenario is also higher, with 25%, 15%, and 10% during the next three years.

Software operating margins would reach 35.5% (compared to the 34.5% in the base case) given a faster revenue growth at 7% per year.

Applying an 8.5% discount rate, a 30x FCF exit multiple, and a 4% long-term growth rate, the average fair price per share would be at $3,150, showing a significant discount to the current price of $1,800.

Management has been asked many times about what price mortgage scores should reach compared to closing costs, and they haven't provided any specific figure so far. What they have commented is that they don't see any elasticity at this point, and I see a real possibility of bringing the prices between 0.5% and 1% of mortgage origination fees.

On the other hand, in a pessimistic scenario, the company would become regulated with a limit on its yearly price increases, something similar to the VeriSign (VRSN) situation. In that case, the growth rate would decrease significantly, alongside the exit multiple and margin expansion, becoming highly dependent on volume growth, which the company has no power to control.

The main question when discounting its future cash flows would be the timing of this happening, although I see a low chance of this happening (10% probability).

If a limit on price increases of 5% was imposed from 2027, assuming a lower growth volume, a 3% B2C revenue growth, and a 3% growth in software revenues, operating margins should also be adjusted.

In this scenario, I apply an 88% operating margin on the score segment and a 30% on the software segment, a tax rate between 22% and 24%, with a 3% growth in perpetuity and a 20x FCF exit multiple, obtaining an average fair price of $730 per share.

Risks

The two main risks I see for FICO's development are the regulation on its price increases, given that I believe the company has pushed prices up fast in a short period, and a change in regulation regarding the requirements for selling loans to the GSEs.

Price Increase Regulation

Even I see low chances of a short-term regulation regarding price hikes given it is a B2B business and the credit bureaus have been able to pass price increases due to the low costs compared to the overall mortgage origination costs, I believe the company has recently attracted some attention.

In October 2024, 34 Democratic senators and representatives submitted a letter urging the previous U.S. President to direct the Department of Justice to investigate FICO.

Given the change in presidency and the newly appointed FHFA president, I believe some of the fears might be dissipating given the deregulation approach of the new government.

GSE Mortgage Score Regulation

The second most relevant risk is the possibility of a change in regulation regarding the requirements to sell a mortgage to the GSEs.

While the FHFA has postponed indefinitely the transition to the bi-merger report and the use of Vantage Score for government-backed mortgages, over the long term there could be the possibility that consumers can choose between the use of one or the other score.

While I don't believe giving total power to the credit bureaus would benefit the industry, since having a third party as FICO providing the score has proven to be efficient, it is a risk worth keeping an eye on.

Conclusions

By assigning a 10% chance on the pessimistic scenario, a 20% on the most optimistic scenario, and a 70% chance to my base case scenario, the current fair price stands at $1,760.

While some risks that could significantly impact FICO's performance if they materialize, I don't assign them a high chance taking into account the mortgage originations market structure and the constant delays from the FHFA in regard to the bi-merge report implementation.

I acknowledge the current overvaluation of the company, but given the high quality of the business and the margin for further price increases over the next years, I will increase my position as the stock falls in the $1,500-$1,700, assigning it a buy rating.

data.title: Meta's Bullish Rally Is Far From Over
data.description:
Meta’s (NASDAQ:META) recent earnings have reinforced the narrative that the company is in a better position to monetize its investments in AI. The diluted EPS grew by a staggering 50% compared to the year-ago quarter. Meta is showing strong revenue growth as well as margin expansion which has helped boost the EPS growth momentum for the company. A key metric to look for in Meta is the YoY growth in price per ad. In the recent quarter, price per ad showed 14% YoY growth which shows better ad targeting by Meta. Good growth in this metric can increase the margin and overall EPS for the company in the next few quarters. In the previous article, it was mentioned that Meta could deliver a strong performance as new AI tools come online.

Meta is also losing close to $20 billion in Reality Labs on an annualized basis. This is a massive investment, and we are seeing new competition from Samsung in addition to Apple’s (AAPL) VR efforts. This segment showed revenue of $1.08 billion, which is a modest 1% YoY growth. Meta will need to increase the sales performance of this segment in 2025 to justify the huge losses and to maintain its market share as new devices are launched in this segment.

Despite the big bull run of the last two years, Meta stock is still reasonably valued when we compare it with other big tech peers like Apple. Meta’s forward PE multiple is 24.2 for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026. On the other hand, Apple is trading at over 28 times the EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Sep 2026. Meta is also showing better revenue and EPS growth momentum than Apple, which should help the stock gain a better sentiment over the next few quarters.

Meta’s rapid monetization of capex

A few days before the earnings, Mark Zuckerberg announced a plan to ramp up capex to a range of $60 billion to $65 billion in 2025. The company is building massive data centers and has 1.3 million GPUs powering its AI tools. The top range of Nvidia’s (NVDA) GPU costs as much as $40,000 which shows the huge investment required to build this much computing power. This announcement came prior to the DeepSeek shock, but the management has reiterated its plans to maintain the current capex trajectory.

Figure: Meta's latest earnings beat. Source: Seeking Alpha

Wall Street has been more positive towards Meta’s capex plans compared to other big tech companies. We recently saw a big correction in Alphabet (GOOG) stock post-earnings, with the higher capex seen as a negative for the stock. Meta has received more positive reviews for its capex plans because of the quick monetization of the AI tools built by the company. These new AI tools are helping Meta deliver more targeted ads to customers which increases the return on investment for advertisers.

Figure: Key metrics for Meta in the recent earnings. Source: Meta filings

The overall revenue for Meta’s Family of Apps increased to $47.3 billion from $39 billion in the year-ago quarter. This is a 21% YoY growth in revenue. Meta reported good growth in all metrics. The daily active people or DAP grew 5% YoY, ad impressions increased by 6% YoY and the average price per ad increased by 14% YoY. Despite the higher investments, Meta managed to increase the margins, which has helped the EPS grow by 50% YoY.

AI impact on price per ad

A key metric to look out for is the price per ad of Meta. Over the last few quarters, Meta has been launching new AI tools for its advertisers. This allows Meta to do better targeting of ads and helps increase the ROI of advertisers. Better ROI for advertisers in turn helps Meta increase the price per ad.

Figure: YoY percentage change in Price per ad for Meta. Source: Meta filings

We can see from the above image that Meta has been able to steadily increase the price per ad percentage change over the last few quarters. In the recent quarter, the price per ad metric showed 14% YoY growth worldwide and 12% YoY growth in the important US & Canada region.

There is an upper limit to how many ad impressions Meta can deliver without making its platforms saturated by ads. However, Meta should be able to increase the price per ad for a long time. Better AI tools and better ROI for advertisers should allow Meta to increase the rates it charges advertisers.

Figure: Increase in capex by Meta. Source: Meta filings

Despite the massive capex announced by the management, Meta could still deliver a good monetization trajectory for these investments. Cheaper and open-source models like DeepSeek should also help improve the return on investment for Meta. The rapid growth of DeepSeek also proves that Meta’s open-source strategy can deliver better results over the long term.

Loss in Reality Labs and new competition

Meta reported another quarter of increase in loss for its Reality Labs segment. The loss for Reality Labs increased from $4.6 billion in the year-ago quarter to $4.96 billion in the recent quarter. Meta is now showing close to $20 billion in annualized losses in the Reality Labs segment. Back in 2014, Meta purchased Oculus for $2 billion, and since then, the company has invested heavily in this segment. On the other hand, the YoY revenue growth for Reality Labs is a mere 1%.

Figure: Revenue and losses in Reality Labs. Source: Meta filings

The losses in this segment could try the patience of most long-term investors. There is also higher competition as Samsung/Google announced the first Android XR headset. The Samsung device, called Project Moohan, is expected to cost under $1,500 making it much more affordable than Apple’s Vision Pro.

It could be a few more years before we could expect the Reality Labs segment to reach anywhere close to breakeven. This is a big risk for the Meta stock, and it is unlikely that the management is going to reduce the current investment scale in this segment. The positive impact of Reality Labs is that it provides Meta with complete control over the device and platform, which it can monetize to a maximum limit through its social media apps. The privacy policies of Apple and other OEMs limit Meta’s monetization ability. Reality Labs will provide devices and platforms that are completely controlled by Meta and this improves the growth runway for the company.

Reasonably priced stock

Over the last two years, Meta stock has increased from under $100 to over $700. This massive growth would seem like the stock is priced for perfection. However, the forward PE multiple of Meta is still quite reasonable when we compare it with other big tech peers. For the fiscal year ending Dec 2025, Meta’s forward PE is 27.9 and for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026 Meta’s forward PE is 24.2.

Figure: Meta’s forward EPS estimate. Source: Seeking Alpha

This is significantly better than Apple and many other big tech peers, which are trading at higher multiples. Apple’s forward PE for fiscal year ending Sep 2026 is 28 which is 20% higher than Meta. We have also seen Meta deliver much stronger revenue growth in the last two years compared to stagnant revenue by Apple. The forward revenue and EPS growth estimates of Meta are much better than Apple, which makes it a more reasonably priced stock at the current price.

Figure: Comparison of Meta and Apple's price and forward EPS.

A lot of future stock price growth in Meta will depend on the ability of the company to effectively use the current investment in AI chips and build better tools for advertisers. If Meta continues to show double-digit price-per-ad growth for the next few quarters, we could see strong bullish momentum in the stock. This could possibly improve the forward EPS estimates, making the stock more attractive.

Investor Takeaway

Meta’s recent earnings report delivered another beat in revenue and EPS. The most important metric for the company is the strong price per ad growth which shows that it is able to do better targeted ads on its platform. We will likely see more AI tools by Meta to help advertisers reach their ideal audience. The capex announcement is quite high by Meta’s management, but Wall Street might accept this rate if Meta could continue to deliver strong revenue and margin growth.

Despite the bullish run of the last two years, Meta stock is reasonably priced when we compare the forward PE multiple of Meta and other big tech peers like Apple. Meta is trading at close to 24 times the EPS estimate for fiscal year ending Dec 2026 which is 20% lower than Apple’s 28 times forward PE multiple.
